blocks.cap: The real work is the whole-number product \(4 \times 2 = 8\), and the only bookkeeping is counting that the factors carried \(3\) decimal places between them, \(2\) from \(0.04\) and \(1\) from \(0.2\). Since \(8\) is a single digit, two placeholder zeros slide it out to the thousandths place so the product reads \(0.008\). That matches eight thousandths from the fraction meaning, \(\tfrac{4}{100}\times\tfrac{2}{10}=\tfrac{8}{1{,}000}\).

blocks.x: Splitting one unit of money into one hundred smaller ones is newer than you might guess. The United States chose it on purpose. In 1792 the brand new country defined the dollar and ruled that it be divided into 100 cents, making it one of the first nations to put money on a clean base-ten footing. Britain held out far longer. For centuries a pound was 20 shillings and a shilling was 12 pence, so a pound was 240 pence, and doing money sums meant juggling twelves and twenties in your head. Britain finally switched to 100 pence per pound in 1971, a changeover so large it was simply called Decimal Day. So the tidy two digits after the point on a price tag are not an accident of arithmetic. They are the world slowly agreeing that counting money in tens and hundreds, the same hundredths column you have been lining up, is far less of a headache.

Dividing by a decimal feels a little awkward, so the trick is to make the divisor a friendly whole number first. Say you have a length of rope that is \(4.5\) m and you cut it into pieces each \(0.5\) m long. The number of pieces is \(4.5 \div 0.5\). Write that as a fraction, \(\tfrac{4.5}{0.5}\), and multiply the top and bottom by \(10\). Back in Chapter 4 you saw that multiplying by \(\tfrac{10}{10} = 1\) gives an equivalent fraction, so the value does not budge, and from 5.1 you know multiplying by \(10\) just slides each point one place to the right. The problem becomes \(\tfrac{45}{5}\), a clean whole-number division. The point is you slide both numbers the same amount, so the answer stays exactly the same. How many \(0.5\) m pieces do you get, that is \(4.5 \div 0.5\)?
hints: The divisor is \(0.5\), which has one decimal place. Slide the point one place to the right in both numbers so the divisor becomes a whole number. What do \(4.5\) and \(0.5\) turn into?
Sliding both points one place gives \(45 \div 5\). Now it is plain whole-number division, and \(5\) goes into \(45\) exactly how many times?
blocks.sol: Start by writing the division as a fraction, \(\tfrac{4.5}{0.5}\). To make the divisor whole, multiply the top and bottom by \(10\), which is the same as multiplying by \(\tfrac{10}{10} = 1\), so the value cannot change. Multiplying by \(10\) slides each point one place right, giving \(\tfrac{45}{5}\). Now it is a clean whole-number division, and \(5 \times 9 = 45\), so \(45 \div 5 = 9\). Notice the slide moved both numbers by the same amount, which is exactly why the answer matches the original. A \(4.5\) m rope cut into \(0.5\) m pieces gives \(\boxed{9}\) pieces.

blocks.x: Try a trickier one where both numbers carry a point, and this time the answer will not be a whole number. A ribbon \(1.44\) m long is cut into strips each \(1.2\) m long, which asks for \(1.44 \div 1.2\). Write it as \(\tfrac{1.44}{1.2}\). The divisor \(1.2\) has one decimal place, so slide both points one place to the right by multiplying top and bottom by \(10\), which keeps the value the same. The bottom \(1.2\) becomes the whole number \(12\), and the top \(1.44\) becomes \(14.4\). Now you are doing \(14.4 \div 12\), a decimal divided by a whole number, ordinary division with the point brought straight up into the quotient. Always make the divisor whole, never the other way around, that is the whole point of the slide. What is \(1.44 \div 1.2\)?
hints: Multiply both \(1.44\) and \(1.2\) by \(10\) so the divisor turns whole. That leaves you with \(14.4 \div 12\), which has the same value but is much friendlier.

Divide \(14.4\) by \(12\). Since \(12\) goes into \(14\) once with \(2.4\) left, and \(12\) goes into \(2.4\) exactly \(0.2\) times, the quotient is \(1.2\). Bring the decimal point straight up and you land on \(1.2\).
blocks.sol: Start from \(\tfrac{1.44}{1.2}\). The divisor has one decimal place, so multiply the top and the bottom by \(10\). The bottom becomes \(12\) and the top becomes \(14.4\), so the problem is now \(14.4 \div 12\) with the same value as before. Divide a decimal by a whole number by keeping the point in place and carrying it straight up. Twelve fits into \(14\) once, leaving \(2.4\), and twelve fits into \(2.4\) exactly \(0.2\) times, so the quotient is \(1.2\). You can check it the easy way, since \(1.2 \times 1.2 = 1.44\). So \(1.44 \div 1.2 = \boxed{1.2}\).

blocks.x: So dividing by a decimal isn't really a new trick. I slide both numbers to the right by the same number of places, just enough to make the divisor whole, and then I divide the plain way I already know. Sliding both by the same amount is just multiplying the top and bottom of the fraction by the same power of ten, so it stays an equivalent fraction and the quotient never changes. And now all three moves rhyme. Line up the points to add and subtract, count the places to multiply, slide the point to divide.

Time to put both moves in one problem. A maker buys \(3\) spools of copper wire, each holding \(1.2\) m, and the build in front of them needs \(2.75\) m. They want to know how much wire will be left coiled up once the build is done. Work it in two honest steps. First find how much wire is on hand, which is \(3\) whole spools times \(1.2\) m each. Then take away the \(2.75\) m the build uses. How many metres of wire are left over?
hints: Two clean steps. Step one is a whole number times a decimal, so multiply \(3 \times 1.2\) to get the total length on hand. Step two is a subtraction, the total minus the \(2.75\) m used.

Multiply \(3 \times 12 = 36\), and since \(1.2\) has one decimal place the total carries one place too, giving \(3.6\) m. Now line up the points and subtract \(3.60 - 2.75\), padding \(3.6\) to \(3.60\) so both numbers show hundredths.
blocks.sol: Step one, find the wire on hand. Three spools of \(1.2\) m each is a whole number times a decimal, so multiply the digits and then place the point. \(3 \times 12 = 36\), and \(1.2\) carries one decimal place, so the answer carries one place too. That gives \(3 \times 1.2 = 3.6\) m on hand. Step two, take away what the build uses. Line up the decimal points and pad \(3.6\) to \(3.60\) so both numbers show hundredths. $$3.60 - 2.75 = 0.85$$ So \(0.85\) m of copper wire is left coiled up. \(\boxed{0.85}\)

review.x: A coral reef survey logs two stretches of healthy reef along a transect line. The first stretch measures \(4.3\) meters and the second measures \(5.6\) meters. Line up the decimal points and add to find the total length of healthy reef. What is \(4.3 + 5.6\)?
hints: Stack the numbers so the decimal points sit directly above each other. Both already show one decimal place, so no padding is needed here.
Add the tenths first, \(3 + 6 = 9\) tenths, then add the ones, \(4 + 5 = 9\). Keep the decimal point in the same column.
review.sol: Line up the decimal points so each place value sits in its own column. Both numbers already show one decimal place, so nothing needs padding. Add the tenths, \(3 + 6 = 9\) tenths. Add the ones, \(4 + 5 = 9\). Bring the decimal point straight down into the answer. That gives \(4.3 + 5.6 = 9.9\). The total length of healthy reef is \(\boxed{9.9}\) meters.

review.x: A tall stack of measuring cups holds \(7.2\) liters of water. You pour out \(3.85\) liters to fill a watering can. To subtract neatly, line up the decimal points and pad the top number so both have the same number of decimal places, which turns \(7.2\) into \(7.20\). How many liters are left in the stack?
hints: Rewrite \(7.2\) as \(7.20\) so it has the same two decimal places as \(3.85\), then stack them with the points lined up.
Start from the rightmost column. Since \(0\) is smaller than \(5\), borrow from the tenths place before subtracting.
review.sol: Pad the top number so both have two decimal places, giving \(7.20 - 3.85\). Line up the decimal points and subtract column by column from the right. In the hundredths place \(0\) is less than \(5\), so borrow from the tenths, making it \(10 - 5 = 5\). The tenths place is now \(1\), and \(1\) is less than \(8\), so borrow from the ones, making it \(11 - 8 = 3\). The ones place is now \(6\), and \(6 - 3 = 3\). Bring the decimal point straight down to get \(3.35\). So the stack has \(\boxed{3.35}\) liters left.

review.x: A delivery drone takes off with \(6\) full units of battery charge. The trip drains \(2.35\) units. The first number is a whole number with no decimal point showing, so write it as \(6.00\) to give it the same two decimal places, then stack the numbers with their points aligned and subtract. How many units of charge are left?
hints: Rewrite \(6\) as \(6.00\) so it has a tenths and a hundredths digit, then line the decimal points up with \(2.35\) underneath.
Subtract right to left, borrowing across the zeros. The hundredths borrow to give \(10 - 5 = 5\), the tenths borrow to give \(9 - 3 = 6\), and the ones give \(5 - 2 = 3\).
review.sol: The only trick is giving the whole number a decimal part to subtract from. Write \(6\) as \(6.00\), the same value with two decimal places, and stack it over \(2.35\) with the points aligned. $$\begin{array}{r} 6.00 \\ -\,2.35 \\ \hline \end{array}$$ Work right to left. The hundredths column needs \(0 - 5\), so borrow to get \(10 - 5 = 5\). The tenths column is now \(9 - 3 = 6\) after the borrow cascades, and the ones column is \(5 - 2 = 3\). Bring the point straight down. $$\begin{array}{r} 6.00 \\ -\,2.35 \\ \hline 3.65 \end{array}$$ A quick check, \(2.35 + 3.65 = 6.00\), back to a full battery. So \(\boxed{3.65}\) units of charge are left.

review.x: A square sticker measures \(0.8\) of a centimeter on one side and \(0.4\) of a centimeter on the other. Find the area by ignoring the points, multiplying the digits like whole numbers, then giving the product as many decimal places as the two factors had together. What is \(0.8 \times 0.4\)?
hints: Drop the points for a moment and multiply \(8 \times 4\). That product is the string of digits your answer will use.
Count the decimal places in the two factors. \(0.8\) has one and \(0.4\) has one, so the answer needs two places. Place the point so \(32\) becomes a number with two digits after it.
review.sol: First ignore the decimal points and multiply the digits like whole numbers, so \(8 \times 4 = 32\). Now count how many decimal places the factors had together. The number \(0.8\) has one place and \(0.4\) has one place, which makes two places in all. Give the product two decimal places by writing \(32\) as \(0.32\). So \(0.8 \times 0.4 = \boxed{0.32}\).

review.x: A patch of fog covers \(0.6\) of a window. By noon the fog has thinned, so it now blocks only \(0.5\) of the area it covered before. What fraction of the whole window is still fogged? Give your answer as a decimal.
hints: Taking half of the fogged region means multiplying its size by \(0.5\). So you want \(0.5 \times 0.6\).
Multiply as if they were whole numbers, \(5 \times 6 = 30\), then place the decimal point so the result has as many decimal places as the two factors combined.
review.sol: The fog used to cover \(0.6\) of the window. Now it blocks half of that, so multiply. \(0.5 \times 0.6\). First multiply the digits, \(5 \times 6 = 30\). Each factor has one decimal place, so the product needs two decimal places, giving \(0.30\). Notice it landed below both \(0.5\) and \(0.6\), since taking a part of a part shrinks the amount. \(\boxed{0.30}\)

review.x: A tiny vial of saffron holds \(2.5\) grams, and a recipe calls for \(0.4\) of a vial. To find the grams, ignore the points and multiply as whole numbers, \(25 \times 4\), then place the decimal point by counting how many decimal places the two factors had together. What is \(2.5 \times 0.4\) in grams?
hints: Drop the points and multiply \(25 \times 4 = 100\). Then count the decimal places, one in \(2.5\) and one in \(0.4\), so two places in all.
Place the point two spots from the right of \(100\) to get \(1.00\). Trailing zeros after the point add nothing, so this is a clean whole number.
review.sol: Set the points aside and multiply like whole numbers, \(25 \times 4 = 100\). Now count the decimal places in the factors. There is one in \(2.5\) and one in \(0.4\), two together, so the product needs two decimal places. Counting two spots from the right of \(100\) gives \(1.00\). The trailing zeros after the point are just empty padding, so \(1.00\) is simply \(1\). Here is the small surprise worth keeping. Two numbers that both carry decimals can multiply to a clean whole number, because the decimal places happened to fill in exactly. So \(2.5 \times 0.4 = \boxed{1}\) gram.

review.x: A jeweler measures gold dust on a tiny scale. One reading is \(0.03\) grams and another is just \(0.3\) of that first amount. To find the second mass she computes \(0.03 \times 0.3\). What is the product?
hints: Ignore the decimal points for a moment and just multiply the whole numbers, \(3 \times 3 = 9\).
Now count decimal places. There are two in \(0.03\) and one in \(0.3\), so the answer needs three places. Place the \(9\) so it lands in the thousandths spot, adding zeros to fill the gap.
review.sol: Start with the digits and forget the decimals. Multiplying \(3 \times 3\) gives \(9\). Now count how many decimal places the factors have. The number \(0.03\) has two places and \(0.3\) has one place, for a total of three. The product must therefore have three decimal places. Writing \(9\) with three places means filling in zeros, so \(9\) sits in the thousandths spot as \(0.009\). \(\boxed{0.009}\)

review.x: A division problem keeps the same value even when you slide both numbers the same number of places. Slide the divisor and the dividend in \(7.2 \div 0.9\) one place to the right, which turns the divisor into the whole number \(9\) and the dividend into \(72\). What is \(7.2 \div 0.9\)?
hints: Multiply both \(7.2\) and \(0.9\) by \(10\). The quotient does not change when you scale both numbers the same way.
After sliding, you are computing \(72 \div 9\). Ask how many nines fit into \(72\).
review.sol: Sliding both numbers one place to the right means multiplying each by \(10\), and doing the same thing to both keeps the quotient the same. So \(7.2 \div 0.9\) becomes \(72 \div 9\). Now the divisor is a whole number and the work is easy. Since \(9 \times 8 = 72\), we get \(72 \div 9 = 8\). Therefore \(7.2 \div 0.9 = \boxed{8}\).

review.x: A craft ribbon is \(2.56\) m long. A pattern piece needs a strip \(1.6\) m long. How many of those \(1.6\) m strips' worth of ribbon do you have, in other words how many times as long as one strip is the whole ribbon? Find \(2.56 \div 1.6\).
hints: The divisor \(1.6\) has one digit after the point. Slide both numbers one place to the right so the divisor becomes a whole number.
After sliding, you are computing \(25.6 \div 16\). Divide as usual and keep the decimal point lined up in the quotient.
review.sol: Start by making the divisor whole. Slide both points one place to the right, so \(2.56\) becomes \(25.6\) and \(1.6\) becomes \(16\). The quotient does not change, so \(2.56 \div 1.6 = 25.6 \div 16\). Now divide. \(16\) goes into \(25\) once, leaving \(9\). Bring down the \(6\) to make \(96\), and \(16 \times 6 = 96\), so the next digit is \(6\). Keeping the decimal point in place gives \(1.6\). Check by multiplying back, \(1.6 \times 1.6 = 2.56\), which matches the ribbon. So the ribbon is \(\boxed{1.6}\) times as long as one strip.

review.x: A scientist measures two tiny lengths with a laser. The first reads \(0.08\) of a millimeter and the second reads \(0.9\) of a millimeter. To log the total she lines up the decimal points and pads the shorter number so both have the same number of places. What is \(0.08 + 0.9\)?
hints: Pad \(0.9\) to two decimal places so it becomes \(0.90\). Now both numbers reach the hundredths place and the columns line up.
Add straight down. The hundredths column is \(8 + 0\), and the tenths column is \(0 + 9\). Keep the decimal point in the same spot.
review.sol:
Line up the decimal points and pad \(0.9\) so it has two places, giving \(0.90\). The inner zero in \(0.08\) keeps the \(8\) sitting in the hundredths place, so nothing drifts.
Add column by column. Hundredths: \(8 + 0 = 8\). Tenths: \(0 + 9 = 9\). The decimal point stays put.
$$0.08 + 0.90 = 0.98$$ So the total length is \(\boxed{0.98}\) of a millimeter.

review.x: A spool holds wire in short lengths. Each length is \(1.4\) meters, but a wiring kit only needs half of one length. To find half, you multiply by \(0.5\). What is \(1.4 \times 0.5\)?
hints: Count the decimal places before you place the point. One place in \(1.4\) plus one place in \(0.5\) gives two places in the answer.
First multiply the digits as if there were no points, so \(14 \times 5 = 70\). Now place two decimal places, which makes \(0.70\).
review.sol: Ignore the points for a moment and multiply the digits. \(14 \times 5 = 70\). Now count the decimal places in the factors. There is one place in \(1.4\) and one place in \(0.5\), which is two places in total. Place the point so the answer has two decimal places, giving \(0.70\). That matches the idea of taking half of \(1.4\), which is \(0.7\). \(1.4 \times 0.5 = \boxed{0.70}\)

review.x: At a farmers market you buy \(4\) jars of jam at \(0.85\) dollars each and pay with a \(5\) dollar bill. First find the total cost by multiplying \(4 \times 0.85\), then subtract that from \(5\) dollars to find your change. How much change do you get in dollars?
hints: Multiply \(4 \times 0.85\) first. Multiply \(4 \times 85 = 340\), then place the decimal point so there are two digits after it, giving \(3.40\).
Now subtract the cost from \(5\) dollars. Line up the decimal points and write \(5\) as \(5.00\), then compute \(5.00 - 3.40\).
review.sol: Start with the cost of the jam. Multiply \(4 \times 0.85\). Ignoring the point for a moment, \(4 \times 85 = 340\). The factor \(0.85\) has two digits after the point, so the product does too, giving \(4 \times 0.85 = 3.40\). Now find the change. Write the \(5\) dollar bill as \(5.00\) so the points line up, then subtract. \(5.00 - 3.40 = 1.60\). So your change is \(\boxed{1.60}\) dollars.

blocks.x: Look closely at a vending machine sometime and you might catch a number wearing two coats at once. The little card taped beside the slot says the snack costs three quarters of a token, written as the fraction \(\tfrac{3}{4}\). The glowing readout right above it says 0.75 tokens. Nobody snuck over and changed the price between the card and the screen. The snack costs one amount, exactly one, and \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) and \(0.75\) are simply two correct names for that single amount, the way you might go by your full name on a form and a nickname with your friends. A number can wear a fraction coat or a decimal coat, and swapping coats never touches the number underneath. By the end of this lesson you will be switching those coats in both directions, fraction to decimal and back, and doing a lot of it in your head. But before any of that, here is a question I want to leave hanging, because we are not going to answer it yet. Some fractions slide into a neat decimal that just stops, the way \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) settles down into \(0.75\) and goes quiet. Others seem to put up a fight. You start dividing and the digits keep marching out, on and on, refusing to ever land. So what is the secret rule that decides it? What separates the fractions that earn a clean, stopping decimal name from the ones that never sit still? Hold onto that question as you go. The answer is not in the dividing itself. It has been quietly waiting in the prime factors you met back in Chapter 3.

A digital trail sign glows at the start of a hiking path. It reads that the next lookout is \(0.7\) kilometers ahead. You want to record that distance as a fraction in your notebook, since the ranger's logbook only accepts fractions written over a power of ten. Look at where the last digit lands. The \(7\) sits one slot past the point, in the tenths place, so it is counting tenths. There is no whole number out front and no dividing to do here, because a terminating decimal is already a fraction in disguise. The place of the last digit tells you the bottom, and the digit itself is the top. Write \(0.7\) as a fraction over a power of ten.
hints: The first slot to the right of the decimal point has a special name. A single digit sitting there is not seven of something big, it is seven of something small. What is the name of that place, and what number goes underneath when you count that many of them?

The last digit is \(7\) and it lives in the tenths place, so the denominator is \(10\). The digits after the point become the numerator, which here is just \(7\). Put the \(7\) on top and the \(10\) on the bottom.
blocks.sol: Read the decimal out loud the way you would say it and the fraction almost writes itself. You would say "seven tenths," and "tenths" is already the denominator while "seven" is already the numerator. Here is why the place does all the work for you. The point splits whole units from pieces of a unit, and each step to the right cuts the piece into ten times as many parts. The very first slot past the point holds tenths, so a digit sitting there counts how many tenths you have. In \(0.7\) that digit is \(7\), which means you have \(7\) copies of one tenth. That is exactly $$0.7 = \frac{7}{10}.$$ Notice there was no division and no whole part to carry along, since the sign showed less than one kilometer. The single filled place named the denominator, \(10\), and the digit after the point became the numerator, \(7\). So the lookout is \(\boxed{7/10}\) of a kilometer ahead.

blocks.x: Here is the same move you just made, only one place deeper, and this time the fraction comes out baggy. A strip of ribbon measures \(0.45\) of a meter. There are two digits sitting past the point now, so the last filled place is hundredths and the denominator you start with is \(100\). Read the two digits after the point as the ordinary whole number \(45\) and drop it on top, which gives \(\frac{45}{100}\). That answer is correct, but it is not pulled tight, since \(45\) and \(100\) both share a hidden factor. Use the gcd idea from Chapter 4 to take it all the way down. Write \(0.45\) as a fraction in simplest form.
hints: Start where the reading tells you to start. Two places past the point means hundredths, so \(0.45 = \frac{45}{100}\). Now ask yourself the Chapter 4 question. What is the largest number that divides both \(45\) and \(100\) at the same time?

List what divides each one. The number \(45\) splits as \(3 \times 3 \times 5\), and \(100\) splits as \(2 \times 2 \times 5 \times 5\). The only factor they truly share is a single \(5\), so \(\gcd(45,100) = 5\). Divide the top and the bottom by \(5\).
blocks.sol:
Two digits live past the decimal point, so the last place is hundredths and the denominator is \(100\). Reading the digits as a whole number puts \(45\) on top, giving the honest first version \(\frac{45}{100}\). It is true, just loose.
\nTo tighten it, find what \(45\) and \(100\) have in common. Break each one into its prime pieces. \(45 = 3 \times 3 \times 5\) and \(100 = 2 \times 2 \times 5 \times 5\). Lining those up, the only piece showing in both lists is a single \(5\), so the greatest common divisor is \(\gcd(45,100) = 5\). That \(5\) is the biggest cut you can make in one step.
\nDivide top and bottom by it together. $$\frac{45}{100} = \frac{45 \div 5}{100 \div 5} = \frac{9}{20}$$ Now check that nothing is left to remove. The top is \(9 = 3 \times 3\) and the bottom is \(20 = 2 \times 2 \times 5\). They share no prime, so the fraction is pulled all the way tight. If you want proof it is still the same length of ribbon, notice \(\frac{9}{20} = \frac{45}{100} = 0.45\), right back where you started.

\(\boxed{9/20}\)

blocks.x: A terminating decimal is already a fraction, so I never have to divide to turn it into one. The place of the last digit hands me the denominator, tenths sit over \(10\), hundredths over \(100\), thousandths over \(1000\), and every digit after the point, read straight across as one number, becomes the numerator. Reading it that way just sets the fraction down, and then I tidy it the Chapter 4 way by dividing top and bottom by their gcd. That is why \(0.7\) is already \(\tfrac{7}{10}\), while \(0.45\) starts as \(\tfrac{45}{100}\) and trims down to \(\tfrac{9}{20}\).

blocks.cap: The digits after the point pile up into the numerator, and the last place you reach names the denominator. So \(0.125\) reads off as \(\frac{125}{1000}\). Then the \(\gcd\) does the cleanup. Both \(125\) and \(1{,}000\) divide by \(125\), which folds that heavy looking decimal down to the tiny \(\frac{1}{8}\). Notice \(1{,}000 = 8 \times 125\), a thread worth remembering.

blocks.x: Not every decimal hides behind a zero. A scoop on the kitchen scale reads \(2.5\) kilograms of flour, a whole part out front and a tail of \(5\) tenths behind the point. There are two honest ways to read it. You can say two and five tenths, which is \(2 + \tfrac{5}{10}\), or you can read every digit at once as twenty-five tenths, which is \(\tfrac{25}{10}\). Notice that the single digit past the point still puts a \(10\) on the bottom even though a whole number is sitting in front of it. Write \(2.5\) as a single improper fraction in simplest form.
hints: Read all the figures at once, ignoring the point for a moment. The digits are \(25\), and one digit sits past the decimal point, so the bottom is \(10^1 = 10\). That gives you \(\tfrac{25}{10}\) before you tidy it up.

Now reduce \(\tfrac{25}{10}\). Both numbers share a factor of \(5\), so divide top and bottom by \(5\). You get \(\tfrac{25 \div 5}{10 \div 5}\), and since the top stays bigger than the bottom, leave it as an improper fraction rather than a mixed number.
blocks.sol: The cleanest route is to read every figure in \(2.5\) at once and forget that one of them lives in front of the point. The digits are \(25\), and exactly one digit sits to the right of the decimal point, so the denominator is \(10^1 = 10\). That gives \(2.5 = \tfrac{25}{10}\). The whole part out front did not change the rule, the count of digits past the point is still what sets the power of ten underneath. If you prefer the other reading, two and five tenths is \(2 + \tfrac{5}{10} = \tfrac{20}{10} + \tfrac{5}{10} = \tfrac{25}{10}\), the very same fraction. Now simplify. The greatest common factor of \(25\) and \(10\) is \(5\), so \(\tfrac{25}{10} = \tfrac{25 \div 5}{10 \div 5} = \tfrac{5}{2}\). Since \(5\) is larger than \(2\), this is already an improper fraction, exactly what was asked for. A quick sanity check, \(5 \div 2 = 2.5\), back to where we started. $$2.5 = \frac{25}{10} = \boxed{5/2}$$

blocks.x: A weather station logs the overnight temperature change as \(-1.72\) degrees. The minus sign tells you the temperature dropped, and that sign is going to ride along untouched while you do the conversion. Set it aside for a moment and look only at the size, \(1.72\). The last digit sits in the hundredths place, so \(1.72\) reads as one hundred seventy-two hundredths, which is \(\tfrac{172}{100}\). Simplify that fraction to lowest terms, then put the minus sign back on the front exactly where it started. Write \(-1.72\) as a single improper fraction in simplest form, including the sign.
hints: The negative sign is just along for the ride. Convert the size 1.72 on its own. Two decimal places means hundredths, so the bottom of the fraction is 100.
Reduce \(\tfrac{172}{100}\) by its greatest common divisor. Since \(\gcd(172,100)=4\), divide both the top and the bottom by 4, then write the minus sign in front of the result.
blocks.sol:
Handle the sign and the size separately. The negative sign is not a number to be simplified, it is just a label saying which direction the temperature moved, so park it and convert the size \(1.72\) by itself.
\nThe digit \(2\) is the last digit and it lives in the hundredths place, so \(1.72\) is one hundred seventy-two hundredths. As a fraction that is \(\tfrac{172}{100}\).
\nNow reduce. The greatest common divisor of \(172\) and \(100\) is \(4\), so divide top and bottom by \(4\): $$\frac{172}{100}=\frac{172\div 4}{100\div 4}=\frac{43}{25}.$$ Check that this is fully reduced. The numerator \(43\) is prime, and \(25=5\cdot 5\) shares no factor with it, so \(\tfrac{43}{25}\) cannot shrink any further.
\nFinally, put the sign back exactly where it was. The size came out to \(\tfrac{43}{25}\), and the temperature dropped, so the answer is negative. \(\boxed{-43/25}\)

blocks.x: So far you have pushed decimals into fractions. Now turn the trip around and go from a fraction to a decimal. The friendliest possible starting point is a fraction whose denominator is already a power of ten, because then you barely have to do anything. A depth sensor on a small underwater rover reports its position as \(\frac{47}{100}\) of a meter below the surface. The denominator is already \(100\), the hundredths, so the place value is handed to you. You just have to set the digit \(7\) into the hundredths slot, the digit \(4\) into the tenths slot beside it, and guard the empty ones place with a \(0\). Write \(\frac{47}{100}\) as a decimal.
hints: The bottom number tells you the smallest place value in play. A denominator of \(100\) means the last digit of the numerator should land in the hundredths place, two spots to the right of the decimal point.

Take the numerator \(47\). The \(7\) goes in the hundredths place and the \(4\) goes in the tenths place. There is no whole part, so write a \(0\) in the ones place to hold it open, then the decimal point, then \(47\).
blocks.sol:
Remember from earlier that \(\frac{47}{100}\) just means \(47\) copies of one hundredth, and one hundredth is exactly what the second place after the decimal point measures. So the whole job is to write \(47\) hundredths in place-value form.
The numerator \(47\) has two digits, and the denominator \(100\) carries two zeros. That match is the whole trick. The two zeros tell you to reserve two slots to the right of the decimal point, the tenths and the hundredths, and the numerator fills them right to left. The \(7\) drops into the hundredths place and the \(4\) sits to its left in the tenths place.
Nothing is left over for the ones place, since \(47\) is less than \(100\), so the number is smaller than one whole. We park a \(0\) in the ones place as a guard, just so the decimal point is not floating alone at the front. That gives \(0.47\).
This is exactly the chart you built before, read backward. Going from a decimal to a fraction, you saw \(0.47\) and named it \(\frac{47}{100}\). Going from a fraction to a decimal, you see \(\frac{47}{100}\) and name it \(0.47\). Same chart, opposite direction. $$\frac{47}{100} = \boxed{0.47}$$

blocks.x: A polishing gear in a clock spins through \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) of one full turn before it clicks to a stop. You want to log that motion as a decimal, but \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) does not arrive ready to read. A decimal wants a power of ten underneath it, like tenths or hundredths, and a \(5\) on the bottom is not a power of ten. Here is the lucky part. A \(5\) sits just one short step from \(10\), since \(5 \times 2 = 10\). Nudge the denominator up onto that power of ten and the digits will fall into place. Write \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) as a decimal.
hints: You cannot just swap the \(5\) for a \(10\) on its own, because that would change the value. Whatever you do to the bottom you must also do to the top. What single number turns \(5\) into \(10\) when you multiply by it?

Multiply both \(3\) and \(5\) by \(2\). That gives \(\tfrac{6}{10}\), which is the same value as \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) since you only multiplied by \(\tfrac{2}{2}\), which equals \(1\). Six tenths reads straight off the tenths place.
blocks.sol: The whole game is getting a power of ten under the fraction so the decimal can be read directly. Right now the denominator is \(5\), and \(5\) is one short multiplication away from \(10\), since \(5 \times 2 = 10\). So multiply the bottom by \(2\). The catch is that touching only the bottom would change the value, so multiply the top by \(2\) as well. Doing the same thing top and bottom is just multiplying by \(\tfrac{2}{2}\), and \(\tfrac{2}{2} = 1\), so the value is untouched. This is the equivalent-fraction move from Chapter 4. $$\frac{3}{5} = \frac{3 \times 2}{5 \times 2} = \frac{6}{10}$$ Now the denominator is a power of ten. Six tenths is exactly what the first place after the decimal point holds, so you read it straight off as \(0.6\). The gear turned through \(0.6\) of a revolution. \(\boxed{0.6}\)

blocks.cap: Here is the whole move in one picture. Find the number that turns the denominator into a power of ten, multiply the top and bottom by it, then read the digits straight off into their places. The bottom is \(20\), and \(20 = 2^2 \times 5\), so it is only missing one more \(5\) to become \(2^2 \times 5^2 = 100\). Multiplying top and bottom by \(\tfrac{5}{5}\) keeps the value the same, and it sends \(\tfrac{11}{20}\) to \(\tfrac{55}{100}\). Once the denominator is \(100\), the \(5\) and \(5\) just drop into the tenths and hundredths columns to give \(0.55\).

blocks.x: A scoop of trail mix is \(\tfrac{11}{20}\) raisins by weight, and the rest is nuts and seeds. You want to write that fraction as a decimal so you can compare it against the labels on other bags. The move that worked before was turning the bottom into a power of ten, but 20 is not one, and trying to shrink it down to 10 is hopeless since 10 is smaller than 20. So look the other way. There is a power of ten that 20 divides into evenly, and once you spot it the multiplier almost names itself. Write \(\tfrac{11}{20}\) as a decimal.
hints: You cannot reach 10, so aim higher. What is the next power of ten after 10, and does 20 fit into it a whole number of times?
Since \(20 \times 5 = 100\), multiply both the top and the bottom by 5. That gives \(\tfrac{55}{100}\), and a denominator of 100 reads straight off as hundredths.
blocks.sol: The goal is to give the denominator a value of \(10\), \(100\), \(1{,}000\), or some other power of ten, because those are exactly the denominators that read off cleanly as decimals. Twenty is not one of them, so the question becomes which power of ten it divides into evenly. It will not fit into \(10\), since \(10\) is smaller than \(20\). The next one up is \(100\), and \(100 \div 20 = 5\) with nothing left over. That is how we knew to reach for \(5\). The bottom has to grow from \(20\) to \(100\), and \(5\) is the number that carries it there. Whatever we do to the bottom we must also do to the top, so multiply both by \(5\). $$\frac{11}{20} = \frac{11 \times 5}{20 \times 5} = \frac{55}{100}$$ Now the denominator is \(100\), so the fraction is \(55\) hundredths, which is \(0.55\). A quick check makes sure that fits. We know \(\tfrac{10}{20} = \tfrac{1}{2} = 0.5\), and \(\tfrac{11}{20}\) is one twentieth more than that, so a value just past \(0.5\) is exactly what we should land on. A little over half the scoop is raisins. \(\boxed{0.55}\)

blocks.x: To turn a fraction into a decimal, scale it up until the bottom is a power of ten. Ask what the denominator needs to be multiplied by to land on \(10\), \(100\), or \(1{,}000\), then multiply the top by that same number so the value never changes. Once the bottom is a power of ten, the decimal is just the top read into its places, like \(\tfrac{3}{4}=\tfrac{75}{100}=0.75\). Sometimes the bottom is already a power of ten, sometimes it is one small step away, but scaling up and reading off the digits is the whole forward direction.

Here is where the prime view from Chapter 3 earns its keep. A wrench in a tool drawer is stamped \(\tfrac{7}{8}\) of an inch wide. To read that size off a decimal ruler, you want it written as a decimal. Look hard at the denominator. The number \(8\) is \(2\times 2\times 2\), which is \(2^3\), three 2s and not a single 5 anywhere. A power of ten is built from 2s and 5s in matched counts, since \(10 = 2\times 5\). So this denominator is lopsided, all 2s and no 5s, and that is exactly the gap you have to close. Write \(\tfrac{7}{8}\) as a decimal.
hints: Decimals are just fractions whose denominators are powers of ten. So aim the bottom at a power of ten. You already have three 2s in the \(8\). How many 5s would you need to add so the 2s and 5s match up and form \(10\times 10\times 10\)?

To turn \(8 = 2^3\) into \(1{,}000 = 2^3\times 5^3\) you are missing exactly three 5s, and \(5^3 = 125\). Multiply the top and bottom of \(\tfrac{7}{8}\) by \(125\), then read off the three decimal places.
blocks.sol: The trick to turning a fraction into a decimal is to get a power of ten on the bottom, because \(\tfrac{875}{1000}\) is just another way of writing \(0.875\). So the whole job is reshaping the denominator into a power of ten without changing the value of the fraction. First break the bottom into primes. Here \(8 = 2\times 2\times 2 = 2^3\), which is three 2s and zero 5s. Now recall how powers of ten are built. Since \(10 = 2\times 5\), every power of ten needs its 2s and 5s in equal counts. For example \(1{,}000 = 10^3 = 2^3\times 5^3\), three of each. Compare that to what you have. Your denominator already supplies all three 2s, so it is short exactly three 5s, and \(5^3 = 125\). Supply the missing 5s by multiplying top and bottom by \(125\), which is the same as multiplying by \(1\), so the value does not change. $$\frac{7}{8} = \frac{7\times 125}{8\times 125} = \frac{875}{1000}.$$ A denominator of \(1{,}000\) means three decimal places, so \(\tfrac{875}{1000}\) reads as \(0.875\). The big idea is that you only ever have to top up whichever prime the bottom is short of. An all-2s denominator like \(8\) cleanly becomes a decimal precisely because you can fill in the matching 5s. \(\boxed{0.875}\)

blocks.x: Topping up to a power of ten was really a game about 2s and 5s all along, because a power of ten is just a stack of 2s matched one for one with the same number of 5s. So scaling the bottom up never invents anything, it only supplies the partners that are missing, the way \(8\) with its three 2s was begging for three 5s, while \(40\) already owns one 5 and only needs two more. The denominator's prime parts decide everything, and the multiplier I reach for is exactly whatever 2s and 5s would finish the matched set. Which makes me wonder about a bottom that smuggles in some other prime, since no pile of 2s and 5s I add will ever cancel it out.

Here are four fractions, each already in lowest terms. Three of them can be turned into a terminating decimal, and exactly one of them cannot. \(\dfrac{7}{16}\), \(\dfrac{9}{40}\), \(\dfrac{5}{12}\), \(\dfrac{3}{25}\). The trick is not to do any dividing yet. Instead, break each denominator into its prime factors the way you learned in Chapter 3, and remember that every power of ten is made of nothing but 2s and 5s, since \(10^n = 2^n \times 5^n\). A fraction becomes a terminating decimal only when you can scale its denominator up to a power of ten, and that is possible only when the denominator is built from 2s and 5s alone. Factor the four denominators and find the odd one out. Which fraction cannot be written as a terminating decimal? Give its denominator as your answer.
hints: Factor each denominator into primes. \(16 = 2^4\), \(40 = 2^3 \times 5\), \(25 = 5^2\), and \(12 = 2^2 \times 3\). Now look for any prime that is not a 2 or a 5.

A power of ten is \(2^n \times 5^n\), so it never contains a factor of 3. Three of the denominators use only 2s and 5s, so you can supply whatever is missing and climb to \(10\), \(100\), or \(1{,}000\). The denominator that hides a 3 can never be scaled into a power of ten, because no multiplier of 2s and 5s will ever cancel that 3.
blocks.sol: Factor every denominator into primes and the answer almost reveals itself. \(16 = 2^4\), so it is pure 2s. \(40 = 2^3 \times 5\), made of 2s and a 5. \(25 = 5^2\), pure 5s. And \(12 = 2^2 \times 3\), which carries a 3. The question is whether each denominator can be scaled up to a power of ten, because a power of ten in the denominator is exactly what a terminating decimal looks like. Since \(10^n = 2^n \times 5^n\), a power of ten is built from 2s and 5s and nothing else. For \(\dfrac{7}{16}\), multiply top and bottom by \(5^4\) to fill in the missing 5s and you reach \(10^4\), so it terminates. For \(\dfrac{9}{40}\), one extra factor of \(5^2\) balances the 2s and 5s into \(1{,}000\). For \(\dfrac{3}{25}\), multiply by \(2^2\) and you land on \(100\). But \(\dfrac{5}{12}\) is stuck. Its denominator holds a 3, and no matter how many 2s or 5s you multiply on, that 3 never disappears, so the denominator can never become a power of ten. The fraction that cannot terminate is \(\dfrac{5}{12}\), and its denominator is \(\boxed{12}\).

blocks.x: So here is what we found. Once a fraction is in lowest terms, look at what its denominator is made of. If the only primes hiding in there are 2s and 5s, you can always feed it the missing 2s or 5s to grow it into a power of ten, and a power of ten is exactly what a terminating decimal is. That is why those fractions stop. But the moment any other prime sneaks in, a 3 or a 7 or an 11, no amount of 2s and 5s can ever clear it, so no power of ten will ever fit and the decimal is forced to run forever. 2 and 5 are the gatekeepers of a clean decimal name.
